title: Best High-Interest Savings Accounts For International Students In Canada: Complete Guide for Canada - International Students Immigrants (2026)
content:
Quick Answer: International students in Canada can open high-interest savings accounts (HISAs) at major banks, credit unions, and digital banks — earning 3.5%–5.5% annually on deposits. Top options include EQ Bank, Simplii Financial, and KOHO. You'll need a valid study permit and passport to apply.

Managing money across borders is one of the most immediate financial challenges international students face upon arriving in Canada. Whether you're sending tuition payments from abroad, receiving family remittances, or building an emergency fund, where you park your money matters — and a standard no-interest chequing account quietly costs you hundreds of dollars each year in lost earnings.

According to Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC), Canada hosted over 1 million active international students as of 2024, representing a record-breaking influx of newcomers navigating an unfamiliar financial system. Many arrive without a Canadian credit history, without a Social Insurance Number (SIN), and without prior experience dealing with federally regulated financial institutions overseen by the Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI).

The good news: opening a high-interest savings account as an international student is straightforward, and you do not need permanent residency or Canadian citizenship to qualify. What you do need is a clear understanding of which institutions accept study permit holders, what documentation they require, and how interest is taxed under the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) rules — even for temporary residents.

The Financial Consumer Agency of Canada (FCAC) regulates how financial institutions disclose rates, fees, and account terms, giving consumers enforceable rights regardless of immigration status. Understanding these protections helps you compare accounts confidently rather than accepting whatever the first bank branch offers.

In this guide, you'll find a ranked comparison of the best HISAs available to international students in Canada for 2026, eligibility requirements, tax implications, and practical step-by-step opening instructions.

If you're still setting up your day-to-day banking, start with our guide on the Best Bank Account for Newcomers to Canada. Once you're earning interest income, you'll also want to explore How to Build Credit in Canada as a Newcomer — a critical parallel step for your long-term financial stability in Canada.

1. Quick Overview: High-Interest Savings for International Students

Canada's high-interest savings account (HISA) market offers international students several strong options in 2026, but eligibility requirements, interest rate structures, and fee models vary significantly across providers. Here's what actually matters when comparing accounts.

Top Accounts Compared

EQ Bank Personal Account Earns up to 2.75% interest (1.00% base rate plus a 1.75% bonus, effective June 11, 2026) with no monthly fees and no minimum balance. Operated under Schedule I bank status, fully regulated by OSFI. International students qualify upon obtaining a SIN and a Canadian address. No physical branches, but interac e-Transfers are unlimited and free. CDIC-insured up to $100,000.

Simplii Financial High-Interest Savings Account Currently offering promotional rates for new clients — confirm current rates at Simplii.com, as promotional periods typically run 90–150 days before reverting to base rates (historically 0.40–1.00%). No monthly fees; requires a linked Simplii chequing account. Backed by CIBC, meaning full CDIC deposit protection.

Tangerine Savings Account Offers competitive promotional rates for new clients with no monthly fees. Tangerine is a Schedule I bank (subsidiary of Scotiabank), CDIC-insured. International students with a valid SIN can open accounts online. Automated savings features — like round-ups and scheduled transfers — make it particularly useful for students managing irregular income (stipends, part-time work).

KOHO High-Interest Account KOHO operates as a fintech partnered with Peoples Trust, a CDIC member institution. The KOHO Everything plan offers up to 5% cash back on spending categories plus competitive savings interest, but requires a paid monthly subscription ($19/month). For students primarily focused on savings yield rather than spending rewards, the net return depends heavily on monthly spending volume. Worth calculating breakeven before subscribing.

Wealthsimple Cash Account Earns 1.25% interest for Core clients and 1.75% for Premium clients on Canadian dollar balances (rates tiered by plan, effective 2026). No monthly fees at the base tier. CIPF and CDIC coverage applies through its partner banks. International students can open accounts; identity verification accepts international passports paired with Canadian address documentation.

What International Students Must Verify Before Opening

  1. SIN Requirement: All interest-bearing accounts require a valid SIN for CRA tax reporting — as covered above, your T5 slip reflects annual interest earned, which may affect scholarship taxability.
  1. Residency documentation: Some institutions require proof of Canadian address within 30–90 days of account opening. Student lease agreements or university housing letters satisfy this requirement at most providers.
  1. Non-resident withholding tax: If you spend significant periods outside Canada during the academic year, confirm your residency status with the CRA. Non-residents face a 25% withholding tax on Canadian interest income (reducible under tax treaties depending on home country).
  1. CDIC vs. DPSL coverage: Accounts at federally chartered banks fall under CDIC. Quebec-based credit unions fall under the Deposit Protection Service of Québec (DPSQ). Both provide legitimate deposit protection, but coverage limits and claim processes differ.

Rate vs. Accessibility Trade-Off

Purely chasing the highest advertised rate ignores practical friction. Promotional rates expire; transfer limits restrict liquidity; some fintechs impose holds on large deposits. For international students receiving lump-sum tuition transfers or study permit stipends, a provider with no incoming wire fees and high CDIC coverage matters more than a 0.25% rate difference.

Practical priority order for most international students:

  1. Zero monthly fees
  2. No minimum balance requirement
  3. Competitive everyday rate (not promotional)
  4. Unlimited e-Transfers for rent, tuition installments
  5. Strong mobile app with English and multilingual support

EQ Bank and Tangerine consistently satisfy all five criteria in 2026, making them the baseline comparison for any international student evaluating HISAs in Canada.

2. How High-Interest Savings Accounts Work (and Why Students Need One)

A high-interest savings account (HISA) is a deposit account that pays a materially higher annual percentage yield than a standard chequing or basic savings account — typically structured so interest compounds daily and credits monthly. For international students and newcomers, the functional definition extends beyond the rate itself: the account must be accessible without Canadian credit history, accommodate a temporary SIN (beginning with 9), and remain compliant with FINTRAC's anti-money laundering identification requirements under the Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act.

Why the Standard Savings Account Falls Short

Traditional Canadian bank savings accounts — offered by the Big Six — pay between 0.01% and 0.05% on balances held in basic accounts. On a $5,000 CAD balance (a realistic amount for a semester's living expenses), that yields under $3 annually. A competitive HISA at current market rates generates $150–$200 on the same balance. For students managing tight budgets under study permit restrictions that limit off-campus work hours, that difference is meaningful.

Beyond yield, international students face a structural barrier: most premium savings products from chartered banks require a Canadian credit score, a permanent SIN, or in-branch identity verification that presupposes a provincial ID card. Digital-first institutions have largely eliminated these friction points, which is why the accounts named in Section 1 dominate this segment.

The Regulatory Framework That Protects Your Deposits

Deposits held at CDIC member institutions — as noted above, CDIC insures eligible deposits — are protected under the Canada Deposit Insurance Corporation Act. However, not every account in this guide is CDIC-covered. Prepaid card accounts and accounts held at non-member credit unions fall under provincial deposit insurance schemes, which vary significantly by province. Ontario's Financial Services Regulatory Authority (FSRA) and British Columbia's Credit Union Deposit Insurance Corporation (CUDIC) each carry different coverage limits and eligibility criteria. Students should verify the specific insurance regime before depositing funds above the applicable threshold.

OSFI, which supervises federally regulated financial institutions, requires all account-holding institutions to maintain adequate capital ratios under Basel III standards — providing an additional systemic layer of protection separate from deposit insurance.

Why Immigrants Specifically Need a Dedicated HISA

New permanent residents and international students share a critical financial window: the first 6–24 months in Canada when credit history is being built and financial identity is being established with the CRA. During this period:

  • Emergency reserves earn nothing in chequing accounts. Housing deposits, one-way flights home, medical gaps not covered under the Interim Federal Health Program (IFHP), and tuition installments all require accessible liquid savings. Parking that reserve in a HISA rather than chequing produces compounding returns with no lock-in period.
  • Exchange rate exposure is real. Many students receive remittances from abroad. Holding CAD in a high-yield account reduces the urgency to immediately convert foreign currency at unfavorable rates, providing a buffer to time transfers.
  • Tax residency implications matter. Interest earned in a Canadian HISA is reportable to the CRA as investment income once a student or immigrant establishes Canadian tax residency — typically determined by residential ties under Income Tax Act Section 250. Unlike tax-sheltered accounts (TFSA eligibility requires permanent residency or citizenship), HISA interest is fully taxable. Students should account for this when projecting net yield.
  • No TFSA access during study permit period. International students on a study permit are generally not eligible to contribute to a Tax-Free Savings Account until they become Canadian residents for tax purposes. A HISA is therefore the primary interest-bearing vehicle available to them — making the rate differential between accounts directly impact take-home returns.

The Core Value Proposition

For a demographic that cannot yet access mortgages, investment accounts, or premium credit cards, a well-chosen HISA functions simultaneously as an emergency fund, a currency buffer, and an early relationship with a financial institution that may later extend credit products. The accounts covered in this guide were selected specifically because they meet FINTRAC identification standards, accept temporary SINs where noted, and carry competitive yields without monthly fees that would erode returns for students operating on restricted budgets.

3. Eligibility Requirements for Immigrants

Opening a high-interest savings account in Canada as an international student involves satisfying a specific set of regulatory and institutional requirements. Banks operating under OSFI oversight must verify identity, residency status, and tax obligations before onboarding any non-citizen customer.

Mandatory Documentation

Every federally regulated financial institution in Canada requires the following from international students:

Study Permit A valid study permit issued by IRCC is the foundational document proving legal residency status. Some institutions accept a letter of introduction if the physical permit is pending, but most digital banks require a scannable permit before account activation.

Passport A current, valid passport serves as primary government-issued photo ID. Expired passports are rejected even when paired with a valid study permit.

Social Insurance Number (SIN) As covered earlier, a SIN is required for tax reporting under Canadian federal law. International students receive a SIN beginning with the digit 9, which indicates temporary resident status. Some accounts can be opened with a SIN application in progress, but interest earned cannot be formally reported to CRA until the SIN is issued.

Canadian Address Proof of a Canadian residential address — a lease agreement, utility bill, or university housing confirmation — is required for Know Your Client (KYC) compliance under Canada's Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act obligations, which financial institutions must satisfy regardless of customer nationality.

Enrollment Verification Several institutions — including Simplii Financial and EQ Bank — request proof of enrollment at a Designated Learning Institution (DLI). Acceptable documents include an official enrollment letter or a tuition receipt issued by the institution.

Residency and Age Requirements

International students must be physically present in Canada at the time of account opening. Remote account activation from abroad is not permitted for most products, as FINTRAC regulations require in-person or verified digital identity confirmation from within Canadian jurisdiction.

Age requirements are standardized at 18 years or older for independent account holders. Students under 18 may open accounts jointly with a parent or legal guardian, though this adds complexity around SIN reporting and account control.

Temporary Resident Restrictions

International students are classified as temporary residents, which creates specific constraints:

  • No TFSA access initially: Tax-Free Savings Accounts (TFSAs) require Canadian residency status for contribution room accumulation. International students present in Canada on a study permit do qualify as Canadian residents for TFSA purposes — contribution room begins accumulating the year they turn 18 and are resident — but they should confirm their specific status with a licensed tax advisor, as departure triggers deemed disposition rules.
  • FHSA ineligibility: The First Home Savings Account (FHSA) requires Canadian residency and a Social Insurance Number. International students on study permits typically do not qualify until they obtain permanent residency or meet specific income tax filing thresholds.
  • CDIC coverage applies regardless of citizenship: As referenced earlier, CDIC deposit protection extends to all eligible depositors at member institutions regardless of immigration status, provided deposits are in eligible categories.

Credit History Considerations

International students arrive without Canadian credit history, which can affect eligibility for premium account tiers at some institutions. KOHO and EQ Bank do not require credit checks for standard account access, making them structurally more accessible than traditional bank accounts that may apply internal credit scoring during onboarding.

SIN Digit-9 Reporting Obligations

Students holding a digit-9 SIN must understand that interest income is still reportable to CRA on an annual basis. If a student's study permit expires and is not renewed, the CRA expects a final-year tax filing covering the period of Canadian residency. Failing to file can trigger penalties and complicate future immigration applications.

Meeting these eligibility thresholds positions international students to access the accounts compared in Section 1 without delays from incomplete documentation.

4. Top High-Interest Savings Accounts Reviewed

Six accounts consistently emerge as frontrunners when evaluated on interest competitiveness, newcomer accessibility, and regulatory standing under OSFI and CDIC frameworks.

EQ Bank Personal Account

Best for: Maximizing passive interest with no fees

EQ Bank operates entirely digitally, which reduces overhead and passes savings directly to depositors. The hybrid chequing-savings structure means your balance earns interest on every idle dollar — no minimum balance required to unlock the posted rate. CDIC protection applies as noted above. One limitation: no physical branches, which some newcomers find challenging when resolving identity verification issues tied to IRCC documentation discrepancies.

Key advantage: No monthly fees, no minimum balance, competitive everyday rate without promotional gimmicks.

Simplii Financial High-Interest Savings Account

Best for: Students wanting big-bank infrastructure at digital rates

Backed by CIBC, Simplii gives international students access to a recognizable banking network while maintaining lower-fee digital operations. CDIC coverage applies through CIBC's member standing. The promotional rate structure (already referenced above) rewards early adopters, making it ideal for students who open accounts immediately upon arrival rather than waiting months into their studies.

Key advantage: CIBC ATM network access, strong identity verification process compatible with study permit documentation.

Tangerine Savings Account

Best for: Students who prefer Scotiabank's infrastructure with digital convenience

Tangerine, owned by Scotiabank, offers a promotional introductory rate that applies for a defined period on new deposits. Beyond the promotional window, the everyday rate is more modest — students should calendar-track when promotional periods expire and consider rate-shopping at that point. CDIC member institution.

Key advantage: Scotiabank ATM access, established complaint resolution process recognized by FCAC.

KOHO High-Interest Account

Best for: Students building credit history simultaneously

KOHO is a federally regulated financial technology provider holding deposits with a CDIC-member partner institution. Unlike traditional HISAs, KOHO's credit-building feature — relevant for students without Canadian credit history — distinguishes it structurally. The premium tier carries a monthly fee; students should calculate whether the credit-building benefit justifies that cost versus free alternatives.

Key advantage: Credit-building tool embedded in savings product — rare combination for newcomers with thin credit files.

Wealthsimple Cash Account

Best for: Students comfortable with investment-platform ecosystems

Wealthsimple holds client cash through CDIC-member institutions via a pass-through deposit structure. The account integrates with Wealthsimple's broader investing suite, which appeals to students planning to begin TFSA investing once SIN is confirmed and eligibility established. CIPF protection applies to investment accounts separately — the Cash Account itself relies on CDIC pass-through coverage.

Key advantage: Seamless migration path from savings to registered accounts as immigration status stabilizes.

Peoples Trust Standard Savings Account

Best for: Students prioritizing deposit security with competitive rates

Peoples Trust is a Schedule I federally regulated trust company under OSFI supervision with CDIC membership. Less prominent in marketing than fintech competitors, it attracts students who prioritize regulatory pedigree over app design. Account opening may require in-person or document-heavy verification, which adds friction but adds confidence for risk-averse newcomers.

Key advantage: Strong regulatory standing, consistent rates without reliance on promotional windows.

Side-by-Side Snapshot

⚠️ Important — rates change frequently: The interest rates in this guide are indicative and were last reviewed against each provider’s official website on June 30, 2026. EQ Bank, Tangerine, Wealthsimple, Simplii, Scotiabank, KOHO and Oaken figures were verified directly against each provider’s official rate page; promotional rates in particular expire and revert to lower base rates. Always confirm the current rate on the provider’s official website before opening an account.

Students prioritizing rate purity should focus on EQ Bank or Peoples Trust. Those needing ATM infrastructure gain more from Simplii or Tangerine. Students with no Canadian credit history should seriously evaluate KOHO despite the fee structure.

Best High-Interest Savings Accounts for International Students in Canada: Side-by-Side Comparison

Choosing the right savings account as an international student means balancing interest earnings against monthly fees, minimum balances, and SIN/ITIN requirements. The accounts below are available to temporary residents holding a valid study permit, verified against FCAC's financial institution registry and current institution disclosures (rates as of 2025 — verify directly before applying).

Key regulatory note: All listed institutions operate under OSFI oversight or are registered MSBs subject to FINTRAC compliance. International students must present a valid study permit and temporary SIN (beginning with "9") during account opening. CDIC coverage protects eligible deposits up to $100,000 per category under the Canada Deposit Insurance Corporation Act.

Real-World Examples

Case Study 1: Priya Sharma — University of Toronto, Computer Science Student

Priya arrived from India in September 2023 with CAD $18,000 in living expenses wired from her parents. She opened a EQ Bank Personal Account (then offering 2.50% promotional rate, later 3.00% HISA) within her first week, using her study permit and Indian passport as primary identification — no Canadian credit history required.

Over 12 months, Priya kept an average balance of CAD $6,200 while paying tuition installments from the same account. Her interest earned totalled approximately CAD $186 — money she applied directly toward textbooks. She also avoided $144 in monthly banking fees she would have paid under a traditional Big Five student account. Priya reported the entirely digital onboarding process took under 25 minutes, with no branch visit required — a critical factor given she had no Ontario address established before arrival.

Case Study 2: Carlos Mendoza — McGill University, MBA Candidate

Carlos relocated from Mexico City in January 2024 and initially used a BMO Student Banking account. After consulting a fee-comparison tool on FCAC's official website, he transferred CAD $9,500 into a Simplii Financial High-Interest Savings Account offering 4.60% promotional rate for the first 5 months — generating roughly CAD $168 in interest during that window.

Carlos's study permit and Mexican consular ID satisfied Simplii's identification requirements under FINTRAC's Customer Due Diligence rules. Post-promotion, his rate dropped to 0.40%, prompting him to move funds to Wealthsimple Cash (1.25% Core / 1.75% Premium). His two-account strategy — Simplii for promotional windows, Wealthsimple for ongoing savings — yielded a combined CAD $290 in annual interest, meaningfully offsetting his transit costs for the semester.

Expert Recommendation

Our Top Pick: EQ Bank Personal Account

For international students navigating Canada's banking system, EQ Bank's Personal Account stands out as the strongest overall choice. With a competitive everyday interest rate of up to 2.75% (1.00% base + 1.75% bonus) and no monthly fees, it directly addresses the two biggest pain points for students on tight budgets and limited income.

EQ Bank operates under federal OSFI oversight as a Schedule I bank (a division of Equitable Bank), meaning deposits are CDIC-insured up to $100,000 — a critical trust signal for newcomers wiring international funds. Account opening requires a SIN or ITN, which international students can obtain through CRA, making it fully accessible even before permanent residency.

Key advantages for international students specifically:

  • No minimum balance requirement
  • Free Interac e-Transfers (unlimited)
  • No foreign transaction fees on the Savings Plus Account
  • Full digital onboarding — no branch visit required, important for students without established Canadian credit history

One practical consideration: EQ Bank functions primarily as a digital bank, so students who regularly handle cash should pair it with a no-fee chequing account at a traditional institution such as Scotiabank's Student Banking Advantage Plan.

Real-world note: Newcomers consistently report that EQ Bank's digital-first model reduces friction during the first 90 days in Canada — a period when in-person banking relationships are difficult to establish without a full credit profile.

Runner-Up: Simplii Financial High-Interest Savings Account

Simplii Financial (a CIBC subsidiary, also CDIC-insured) earns runner-up status for international students who want the security of a major bank's infrastructure with competitive savings rates typically ranging 3.00%–5.00% during promotional periods.

Simplii's particular strength is its multilingual client support — a documented advantage for non-English-dominant students — and seamless integration with CIBC ATM networks, providing cash access across Canada without fees.

International students transitioning into the workforce or navigating provincial health coverage gaps should prioritize building emergency savings early. For context on managing financial exposure during those critical first months, our guide on Health Insurance for Newcomers in Canada outlines provincial waiting periods and supplemental coverage costs that directly affect how much liquid savings you need on hand.

Bottom Line

Choose EQ Bank if maximizing passive interest with zero fees is the priority. Choose Simplii if branch-adjacent access and multilingual support matter more. Either account, opened alongside a proper understanding of your full financial obligations in Canada — including healthcare costs detailed in our Health Insurance for Newcomers in Canada guide — positions you to build financial stability from day one.

Frequently Asked Questions: Best High-Interest Savings Accounts for International Students in Canada

What is a high-interest savings account (HISA) and how does it differ from a regular savings account?

A high-interest savings account (HISA) offers a significantly higher Annual Percentage Yield (APY) than a standard savings account. Traditional bank savings accounts in Canada typically offer 0.01%–0.05% interest, while HISAs currently offer 3.00%–5.50% APY depending on the institution. Licensed federally regulated institutions — overseen by the Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI) — provide HISA products insured by the Canada Deposit Insurance Corporation (CDIC) up to $100,000 per depositor category. For international students managing limited tuition budgets, even modest interest earnings meaningfully offset living expenses.

Can international students in Canada open a high-interest savings account?

Yes. International students holding a valid Study Permit issued under the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA) are legally eligible to open bank accounts at Canadian federally regulated financial institutions. According to the Financial Consumer Agency of Canada (FCAC), banks cannot refuse account applications solely based on immigration status. You typically need your study permit, passport, and proof of enrollment. Some digital banks and credit unions have simplified this process further, offering online account opening within 24–48 hours.

Which documents are required to open a HISA as an international student?

Most federally regulated banks and licensed financial institutions require:

  • Valid passport (primary government-issued ID)
  • Canadian Study Permit (IRCC-issued)
  • Letter of Enrollment from your Designated Learning Institution (DLI)
  • Canadian address (campus residence letter accepted)
  • Social Insurance Number (SIN) — obtained from Service Canada, required for tax reporting under the Income Tax Act

Some institutions, including EQ Bank and Simplii Financial, allow account opening without a SIN initially, with a requirement to provide it within 60–90 days of account activation.

What interest rates can international students realistically expect from Canadian HISAs?

As of 2024–2025, competitive HISA rates in Canada range from 3.00% to 5.50% APY. According to Ratehub.ca market data, EQ Bank's Personal Account offers up to 2.75% on everyday balances, while promotional rates from institutions like Simplii Financial offers a 4.60% promotional rate for 5 months, while Oaken Financial offers a competitive 2.80% everyday rate with no promotional expiry. The Bank of Canada's policy rate directly influences HISA rates — when the overnight rate decreases, savings rates typically follow within 30–60 days. Students should compare both promotional and ongoing rates before committing.

Are HISA earnings taxable for international students in Canada?

Yes. Interest income earned in a Canadian HISA is taxable under the Income Tax Act (Canada). The Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) requires financial institutions to issue a T5 Statement of Investment Income for interest earnings exceeding $50 annually. International students who are Canadian tax residents — determined by residential ties, not immigration status — must report HISA interest on their T1 General return. Students earning under the basic personal amount (~$15,705 for 2024) generally owe no federal tax. Consulting a licensed tax professional familiar with Canada-country tax treaties is strongly advised.

Is my money safe in a Canadian HISA as an international student?

Deposits in HISAs held at CDIC member institutions are insured up to $100,000 per depositor per deposit category under the Canada Deposit Insurance Corporation Act. As of 2024, there are 87 CDIC member institutions. Credit union deposits are protected provincially — for example, the Deposit Guarantee Corporation of Manitoba provides unlimited coverage, while the Financial Services Regulatory Authority (FSRA) of Ontario covers up to $250,000. International students should verify their institution's regulatory status before depositing. Digital banks like EQ Bank are CDIC members, providing equivalent federal protection.

What are the best HISAs specifically suited for international students in Canada?

Based on current rates, fee structures, and accessibility for newcomers:

  • EQ Bank Personal Account — up to 2.75% interest, no monthly fees, CDIC insured
  • Simplii Financial HISA — competitive promotional rates, no fees, CIBC-backed
  • Tangerine Savings Account — accessible app, promotional welcome rates
  • Oaken Financial — competitive 2.80% everyday rate with no promo expiry, CDIC insured
  • Scotiabank StartRight® Program — specifically designed for newcomers and students, includes bundled banking

Newcomers consistently report EQ Bank and Simplii Financial as the most accessible options requiring minimal initial documentation.

Can international students open a Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) instead of a HISA?

International students who are Canadian tax residents aged 18+ with a valid SIN are eligible to open a Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) under the Income Tax Act. TFSA contribution room accumulates at $7,000 annually (2024 limit). Interest earned inside a TFSA is completely tax-free, making it more advantageous than a standard HISA for eligible students. However, students who leave Canada permanently may face tax implications on TFSA holdings under their home country's tax rules. The CRA's My Account portal tracks individual TFSA contribution limits and history.

Are there any monthly fees international students should watch for with HISAs?

Many digital HISAs in Canada charge zero monthly maintenance fees, which is particularly advantageous for budget-conscious international students. However, students should watch for:

  • Transaction fees on excessive withdrawals (some accounts limit free withdrawals to 1–2/month)
  • Interac e-Transfer fees at certain institutions
  • Inactivity fees after 12–24 months of dormancy
  • Wire transfer fees for sending money internationally (typically $15–$45 CAD per transaction)

The FCAC's free comparison tool at canada.ca/en/financial-consumer-agency allows side-by-side fee comparisons across licensed institutions, helping students avoid unexpected charges.

How do Canadian HISA interest rates compare to savings accounts in students' home countries?

This varies significantly by country of origin. According to World Bank data, savings rates in countries like India (3.0%–7.0%), Nigeria (4.0%–10.0%), and China (0.35%–1.75%) differ substantially from Canadian HISA rates. For students from low-rate economies like China, Japan, or the European Union, Canadian HISAs at 4.00%–5.50% represent considerably higher returns. Conversely, students from high-inflation economies may find Canadian rates comparatively modest. Currency exchange risk also applies — CAD-denominated interest gains can be offset by unfavorable CAD/home currency exchange rate movements at remittance time.

Can international students send HISA earnings back to their home country?

Yes. International students can transfer HISA earnings internationally through several regulated channels. Federally regulated banks process international wire transfers under FINTRAC (Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada) anti-money laundering oversight per the Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorist Financing Act. Licensed Money Services Businesses (MSBs) such as Wise, Remitly, and Western Union offer lower-cost alternatives — often 60–80% cheaper than bank wire fees according to World Bank Remittance Prices data. Students should retain transfer records for both Canadian CRA and home-country tax compliance purposes.

What happens to a HISA when an international student leaves Canada after graduation?

Upon departure, international students have several options. Accounts can generally remain open while assets exist, though non-resident withholding tax of 25% on interest income (reducible under tax treaties) applies under the Income Tax Act once Canadian residency is lost. Students must notify their financial institution of their non-resident status — failure to do so constitutes a tax compliance violation. Alternatively, accounts can be closed with funds transferred internationally. If the student transitions to a Post-Graduation Work Permit (PGWP) or Permanent Residency, full resident tax status and TFSA eligibility are maintained. Consulting a licensed cross-border tax advisor before departure is strongly recommended.

Conclusion

Choosing the right high-interest savings account as an international student in Canada directly impacts how much your money grows while you focus on your studies.

The accounts reviewed in this guide offer competitive interest rates, low or no monthly fees, and features designed for newcomers navigating the Canadian banking system for the first time. Whether you prioritize a fully digital experience, branch access, or a promotional rate to maximize early deposits, a strong option exists for your situation.

Key takeaways to carry forward:

  • Verify eligibility requirements before applying — some accounts require a Canadian address, SIN, or study permit documentation
  • Compare promotional vs. ongoing rates — a high introductory rate that drops significantly after 3–5 months may not serve you long-term
  • Confirm CDIC or DICO deposit insurance coverage to ensure your funds are protected up to applicable limits
  • Monitor OSFI-regulated institution status when selecting a federally chartered bank or trust company
  • Reassess annually — interest rate environments shift, and the best account in 2025 may not lead in 2026

According to FCAC data, Canadians who actively compare savings products earn measurably more on idle deposits than those who default to their primary chequing institution.

Opening a high-interest savings account is one of the smartest, lowest-effort financial moves available to international students in Canada. Start early, compare thoroughly, and let compound interest work in your favor from day one.
